g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of CPU Scheduling Criteria. These are the essential metrics for evaluating operating system performance.
g.text: Metrics for Evaluating CPU Scheduling Algorithms

g.data-narration: When multiple processes compete for CPU time, the scheduler must decide which one runs next. But how do we decide which algorithm is best?
g.text: In a multiprogramming system, the CPU must choose which process from the ready queue to run next.
The scheduling algorithm determines system performance—but how do we measure "good"?

g.data-narration: We evaluate schedulers using six key criteria: CPU Utilization, Throughput, Turnaround Time, Waiting Time, Response Time, and Fairness.
CPU Utilization measures the fraction of time the CPU is actively executing a process. In real systems, we aim for 40-90% utilization.
g.text: Goal: Keep the CPU as busy as possible, like ensuring an expensive factory machine never idles.

g.data-narration: Throughput measures how many processes complete per time unit. Higher throughput means more work done in less time.
g.text: The number of processes completed per time unit. If CPU is busy, work is getting done.

g.data-narration: Turnaround Time is total time from process submission to completion. A key metric for batch systems, we want to minimize it.
g.text: Total time from when a process submits itself to when it completes execution.
Turnaround Time = Completion Time - Submission Time

g.data-narration: Waiting Time is the total time a process spends in the ready queue. The scheduler only affects waiting time, not execution time.
g.text: Sum of all periods a process spends waiting in the ready queue. The scheduler directly affects this metric.

g.data-narration: Response Time is crucial for interactive systems—the time from request submission to the first output response, not full completion.
g.text: For interactive systems: time from request submission to the FIRST response produced.

g.data-narration: Fairness ensures every process gets CPU time. Without it, starvation occurs—processes indefinitely denied CPU access.
g.text: Every process should get a fair share of CPU time. Starvation: process indefinitely denied CPU access.

g.data-narration: We cannot optimize all criteria simultaneously. Maximizing throughput might increase waiting time—trade-offs are inevitable.
g.text: It's impossible to optimize all criteria at once. Improving one may negatively impact another.

g.data-narration: Different systems prioritize different criteria: Batch systems focus on throughput, interactive on response time, real-time on deadlines.
Let us summarize the six scheduling criteria with their goals: maximize utilization and throughput, minimize times, ensure fairness.
The key takeaways: criteria define performance, trade-offs are necessary, and system type determines which criteria matter most.
g.text: These criteria will help you evaluate FCFS, SJF, Priority, and Round Robin algorithms!
